steps.title: Identify what the question asks
steps.content: The student wants to paraphrase Champ and Freeman's quotation to explain barter's inefficiency. The key passage: "the person with whom you wish to trade must not only have what you want but also want what you have." That describes a mutual alignment of desires — both parties must simultaneously want exactly what the other has.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'According to Champ and Freeman, the inefficiencies of barter economies become apparent when goods are traded between parties without the use of money.': Simply restates the definition of barter without paraphrasing why it is inefficient. ✗
  • Choice B — 'The precise alignment of desires that barter economies require, Champ and Freeman contend, makes them inefficient.': Paraphrases the mutual-want requirement from the quotation as "precise alignment of desires" — exactly what makes barter inefficient. ✓
  • Choice C — 'In a barter economy, Champ and Freeman contend, goods are traded directly between parties without the use of money, leading to inefficiency.': Again only restates the definition of barter; does not explain the mechanism of inefficiency from the quote. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Champ and Freeman argue that inefficient barter economies can become monetary economies if a good becomes a unit of exchange.': Shifts to the second Champ and Freeman quote about barter evolving into monetary economies, not the inefficiency explanation. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
